title: Say it without exploding
description: Honest AND kind — the assertive sweet spot.
summaryPoints: Aim for assertive: say what you need AND respect theirs
Use the I-statement: "I feel ___ when ___, and I'd like ___"
Name the behavior, not their character — so they can actually hear you
Match tone to message: a calm voice keeps their defenses down
Open gently — how a talk starts predicts how it ends

content.title: The thing you didn't say
content.body:
Your roommate borrows your stuff and never asks. The first time, you let it slide. The fifth time, you snap — over a missing charger — and now you look like the unreasonable one.

That's the trap. You can be too soft and swallow it until you blow, or too hard and run people over. There's a third option, and it's a skill: saying the real thing in a way the other person can actually take in.
content.imageAlt: A chat bubble showing how tone changes the message

content.title: Three ways to handle it
content.body:
**Passive:** you bury it. "It's fine, whatever." Then it leaks out later as snark or one big explosion.

**Aggressive:** you steamroll. "You're so selfish." You get heard, but they stop listening.

**Assertive:** you say your need AND respect theirs. "Hey, I need a heads-up before you borrow my stuff." Honest and kind. That's the target.

items.text: "It's fine, don't worry about it" (you're not fine)
"I'd like a heads-up next time, is that doable?"
Going silent and slamming a door instead of talking
"Whatever you want, I don't care" through gritted teeth
"I felt left out when plans changed without me"

content.prompt: Being assertive means being harsh or blunt.
content.explanation: Assertive is honest AND kind. It's the calm middle — not the loud, run-people-over end.

content.title: The I-statement
content.body:
The cleanest tool for saying a hard thing is the I-statement:

**"I feel ___ when ___, and I'd like ___."**

Three moves in one sentence:
- **I feel** — owns your emotion instead of accusing
- **when ___** — names the specific behavior, not their character
- **I'd like** — asks clearly for what you want

Compare "You never listen" with "I feel brushed off when I get cut off, and I'd like to finish my thought." Same issue. Only one is hearable.
content.imageAlt: A balance scale weighing care and honesty

steps: Name the specific thing that triggers it
Ask clearly for what you'd like instead
content.explanation: Feeling, then behavior, then request. Own it, point at the action (not the person), then make the ask.

content.prompt: Match each attack-line to its assertive rewrite.

pairs.right: "I feel rushed when plans change last-minute."
"I'd like us to split the chores this week."
"I'd like to finish my point before you reply."
"I feel alone with this and could use a hand."

content.prompt: Which line is the clean I-statement?
content.context: A friend keeps cancelling on you at the last minute.
options: "You're so flaky, I can never count on you."
"Forget it, it's not a big deal anyway."
"I feel let down when plans get cancelled last-minute, and I'd like more notice."
"Whatever, I'll just stop making plans with you."
content.explanation: It owns the feeling, names the behavior (not their character), and makes a clear ask. The others attack, bury it, or threaten.

content.instructions: One word breaks the rule. Tap it.
content.sentence: An I-statement starts with the word you to keep blame off the table.
content.explanation: Starting with “I feel…” owns your experience; starting with “You always…” puts them on the defensive.

content.title: Tone does the heavy lifting
content.body:
The exact same words land completely differently depending on **tone, volume, and timing**. "We need to talk" can be a warm invite or a threat — your delivery decides.

A calm tone keeps the other person's defenses down so they can actually hear you. A sharp one snaps them straight into fight-or-flight, and now you're arguing about your tone, not the issue.
content.imageAlt: A thermometer cooling from hot down to calm

content.prompt: The middle of a screaming match, over text, is a fine time to raise something big.
content.explanation: When you're flooded or hiding behind a screen, tone gets lost and things escalate. Wait until you're calm and talk in person.

content.title: The soft start-up
content.body:
Relationship researcher John Gottman found that **how a conversation begins predicts how it ends.** Open with an attack and it spirals; open gently and you've got a shot.

That's the "soft start-up": lead with your feeling and a specific ask, not a character verdict.

- Harsh: "You never think about anyone but yourself."
- Soft: "I'm feeling stretched thin — can we figure this out together?"

content.prompt: Best way to open the talk?
content.context: Your sibling keeps eating the food you've clearly labeled as yours.
options: "Are you actually incapable of reading a label?"
"Hey, can we sort out the food thing? It keeps tripping me up."
Say nothing and start hiding your food in your room
"Cool, guess nothing's safe in this house anymore."
content.explanation: A soft start-up opens gently and invites a fix. The attack and the sarcasm both raise their defenses, and silence just lets it fester.

content.title: Say the real thing
content.body:
Being assertive isn't about going quiet to keep the peace, and it isn't about winning. It's about saying the real thing — and saying it in a way the other person can actually hear.

That's the whole skill in one move: pick a calm moment, open soft, own your feeling, name the behavior, make the ask. Honest and kind, at the same time.

Now build one yourself.

content.prompt: Turn the bottled-up blow-up into one clean assertive ask.
content.context: Your manager keeps texting you to cover shifts last-minute, and you're at your limit.

options.why: That accuses them — they'll defend, not listen.

options.text: this job is a total joke
options.why: Trashing the whole thing buries your actual ask under drama.

options.text: when I get asked to cover shifts last-minute
because you never think of anyone else
options.why: "Never" attacks their character instead of naming the behavior.

options.text: could we sort the schedule a week ahead?
so deal with it yourself from now on
options.why: That's a shove, not a request — it ends the conversation.

options.text: but honestly forget it, never mind
options.why: Pulling the ask back is passive — nothing actually changes.

content.explanation: You owned the feeling, named the specific behavior, and made a clear, doable ask — assertive, not aggressive or passive. That's a message your manager can actually say yes to.
